entry.title: Re: How to find authorization roles with an object and specific field values tied together?
entry.content:
Thanks that worked. I used the report "Role by Complex Selection Criteria" there I could define the object I was looking for and it gave me an option to select up to 4 different fields in this object as well as the option to define the values for these. 
I did not see that before or knew about it. 

Thank you very much Jurjen, have a nice day!

entry.title: Re: KO020 "open down payments" blocks CLSD on investment...
entry.content: Hi Raymond, thanks for the pointer to KBA 2130927 and Note 599689 — appreciate you taking the time. I read through both. The cause (a residual special G/L / down payment request item for the vendor on this WBS) lines up with what I'm seeing. The catch is that the KBA is scoped to ECC / on-premise / S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, whereas I'm on S/4HANA Cloud **Public** Edition — so FBL1, F-44, SE16/BSIK and F110 aren't available to run the resolution directly. On the Public side I've already verified there's nothing open via Manage Supplier Line Items (incl. special G/L) and the down payment G/L balance is 0, so it looks like a residual record that the standard apps don't expose. Given SE16/F-44 aren't options here, I'll raise a support case to have the residual down payment record investigated. Thanks again for the steer — it confirmed where to look.

entry.title: Production order Operations scheduled on weekends
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We have maintained 2 days as GR processing and 1 day as Queue time (Routing).

When we ran lead time MRP, production order operation is scheduled on weekends (29th Aug. 2026) as well (screenshot attached). SCAL - weekends are marked as off.

Please suggest if this is std. SAP or any other parameter needs to be checked.

Thank you in advance.

John

entry.title: Re: Where can I see SXI_MONITOR errors after successful r...
entry.content:
Hi

Check qRFC Queues (Most Common Scenario for ECC → PI Communication)

Since outbound XI messages from ECC to PI commonly use qRFC processing,
temporary queue-related issues are often visible in the queue monitoring transactions.

Please verify the following:

Transaction SMQ1 → Outbound qRFC Queues
Transaction SMQ2 → Inbound qRFC Queues

Check for indicators such as:

Queue retry attempts
SYSFAIL entries
WAITUPDA status
CPIC or temporary network communication errors
Queue locks or serialization conflicts
Temporary processing blocks or delayed LUWs

You may find a corresponding queue entry matching the timestamp of the observed 3-minute delay.

Analyze System Logs and Runtime Errors

Review the following transactions around the exact time of the delay:

SM21 → System Log
ST22 → ABAP Short Dumps
SMICM → ICM / Network Communication Logs

Pay special attention to events such as:

Work process restarts
Temporary memory/resource shortages
Short network interruptions
ICM or RFC connectivity issues
Gateway communication errors

These transient issues can trigger an automatic retry mechanism,
which would explain the successful reprocessing after the initial failed attempt.

Rgds
Dino

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 08 - CXL case-when-then operator

entry.title: Re: Issue with Failed Mail Trace in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi habeeb,

Short version: No, there is currently no standard application in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that captures invalid or non-existent recipient email addresses, and the behaviour you observed is expected rather than a defect.

The reason is architectural. In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, outbound email leaves the system through SAP's cloud email infrastructure. The status you see in Monitor Email Transmissions (and the Completed status on the output item) only confirms that the email was successfully handed over from SAP to that mail infrastructure - it does not confirm delivery to the recipient's mailbox. Because SAP has no visibility of what happens after handoff, it still reports Sent or Completed, even when the recipient's mail server later rejects or silently drops the message. This is why a deliberately wrong or non-existent address still shows Sent: the handover itself succeeded; the rejection happens downstream, outside SAP's scope.

On the specific question of bounce/non-existent-address detection: the bounce is not reflected back into the S/4HANA Cloud monitoring app; the bounce message is sent back to the sender's address (or the return-path configured in your mail server), so to monitor these you need to check your mail server logs or configure bounce-handling in your email infrastructure (e.g. Microsoft Exchange, an SMTP provider, or the SAP BTP mail service). In other words, capturing invalid/non-existent addresses is a customer-side mail-infrastructure task, not a function exposed inside the S/4HANA Cloud tenant.

What the in-system monitoring can surface is errors that occur before handover - for example a missing recipient address, or a sender/receiver domain that isn't allowed. These errors occur earlier in the process, where the system refuses to even attempt sending because of missing or misconfigured settings (e.g. the "no recipient email address" case, or sender/receiver domains maintained via SSCUI 101311 / 101312 / 101313). A syntactically valid but non-existent mailbox, however, passes those checks and is handed over normally.

Before concluding it's purely a downstream issue, it's worth confirming the two mandatory email-security settings, because a missing one produces the same "Sent but never arrives" symptom for valid addresses too:

  • The SPF record for the sender domain must be published
  • Mandatory DKIM must be set up.

If either is missing, recipient servers may silently discard the mail. See KBA 3274509 and KBA 3265907, plus the mandatory-DKIM KBA 3223182, and the Setting Up Output Management (1LQ) guide for the exact SPF/DKIM steps.

SAP Practical recommendations:

  • For proactive prevention, keep recipient email data clean in the business partner master data, since the system can only validate format, not existence.
  • For reactive detection of bounces/non-existent mailboxes, implement bounce handling on your own mail relay or mail platform and monitor its logs — that is the only place the NDR actually lands.

Other Reference Links:

  • Monitoring Email Transmissions
  • Troubleshooting Email Output

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: Unable to ALL messages in SAP CLOUD ALM as part of In...
entry.content:
Dear Bhanu,
We would need more information to assist with that issue.
As a general recommendation have a look if you followed all prerequisites and configuration steps according to the public Cloud Systems as well as the Integration and Exception Monitoring.
Here is an entry point with the related links towards the S4/HANA Public Cloud Systems and Integration and Exception Monitoring configuration.
https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal/integration-monitoring/calm-s4cloud.html?anchorId=section_20797479

If this does not help solve your issue I suggest opening a case in SAP Cloud ALM. You can use the Built-In Support directly in Cloud ALM to do so.
Best regards,
Romy
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Unlocking the Practical Value of AI for Procurement: Autonomous Spend Management
entry.content: @frankomare Thank you so much for sharing your perspective on AI in Procurement! As research is telling us, digital transformation programs continue to fail most often because of organizational and operational issues or an even more basic learning, by misaligned transformation goals and overall strategic priorities of the company. This is what I see in my customer engagements @SAPBusinessTransformationManagement.

entry.title: Re: Production order Operations scheduled on weekends
entry.content:
Hello @johnsplit123 

Check your factory calendar settings and verify that weekends are off there. If a calendar is set in the operation's work center, make sure this calendar defines weekends as non-working days. Otherwise, the factory calendar is used.

Consider notes:

  • 3740986 - Factory Calendar Not Considered with Scheduling Type “Only Capacity Requirements”
  • 150007 - Consulting: Scheduling of production orders

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Dynamic Teams from SAP SuccessFactors: What are we hearing from customers?
entry.content:
HI

 

It have been 2,5 years since then, and not much has happened with the Dynamic Team

We can integrate OKR with Performance goal, but they are not really visible, hidden under sub menus.. The module is a great idea, that does not really delves, and nothing happens. Will he module just die (as with the Initaitives) or will something happen? 

* Search for availability? 

* better integration with goal plans

* better visibility into goals for Team leader? 

* Attaching notes and files to Objectives and Key results?/

Christian

entry.title: Re: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code is now available on the VS Code marketplace!
entry.content:
Great progress so far. I've been using the extension daily for ABAP class development, and the experience has been very positive considering this is still an early version.

Once support for running reports, editing DDIC objects, and further debugger enhancements (such as line skipping) are available, VS Code will become a fully viable environment for day-to-day ABAP development.

One small improvement area is object activation. When changing a single method, it currently feels like additional activation steps are needed. While I know multiple objects can be activated at once, the Eclipse experience felt more seamless from a developer's perspective.

Looking forward to the upcoming releases.

entry.title: Re: Simplified Maintenance Resource Scheduling - RSH (43R) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

entry.title: Re: Production order Operations scheduled on weekends
entry.content: @johnsplit123 check my updated response

entry.title: Re: Cloud ALM Custom application Crash
entry.content:
Dear MK,

I believe what you are referring to is the SAP Alert Notification for SAP BTP,  which supports alerting on custom applications in BTP  and other runtime events and which is not related to SAP Cloud ALM.

Also SAP Cloud ALM provide the possibility to monitor custom applications in SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry environment.

Please check out the help page which provides the setup steps as well as the monitoring capabilities of SAP Cloud ALM, in terms of SAP BTP, CF environment.

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Approach for SAP Cloud ALM feature tester authorization requiring role collection activation
entry.content:
In our project our process requires testers being able to set status of Feature artefacts to "Successfully tested" after successful performed functional unit test. 

However, standard CALM setup only lets e.g. project lead, change / deployment manager do this. 
According to SAP documentation for role collection recommendations (https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/role-collection-recommendations) there is a role template which we assume that meets our needs, once activated:

imp_cdm_TesterChange feature status to Successfully Tested

What steps does one have to take to bring this role template to life?
AI came up with procedure proposal below - can you please either confirm or indicate the correct approach?

Proposed approach for SAP Cloud ALM feature tester authorization

To allow selected project users to set Features to “Successfully Tested”, we plan to create a custom role collection in the SAP BTP subaccount where SAP Cloud ALM is subscribed.

Steps

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit.

  2. Go to the relevant subaccount:

 
Global Account → Subaccount with SAP Cloud ALM subscription
  1. Create a new role collection:
 
Security → Role Collections → Create

Example name:

 
CALM_Feature_Tester
  1. Add the required SAP Cloud ALM role templates/roles to this custom role collection:
 
imp_cdm_Tester

This role template should allow users to change the Feature status to Successfully Tested.

In addition, the users also need a role that gives access to the Features app, for example:

 
imp_pjm_ProjectMember

So the proposed custom role collection would contain:

 
CALM_Feature_Tester
- imp_cdm_Tester
- imp_pjm_ProjectMember
  1. Assign the new custom role collection to the relevant users or user group, either:
  • directly in SAP BTP Cockpit, or
  • via group mapping from the configured Identity Provider.

  1. In SAP Cloud ALM, ensure that the users also have access to the relevant project/team.

  2. Ask the users to log out and log in again, then test whether they can open the Features app and set a Feature to Successfully Tested.

Question for confirmation

Is this the correct approach to enable selected SAP Cloud ALM project users to set Features to Successfully Tested using the imp_cdm_Tester role template, combined with a project/member role such as imp_pjm_ProjectMember?

entry.title: Public Cloud 2602 : 43R (Maintenance Resource Scheduling - RSH)
entry.content:
Hello All,

Scope Item: 43R (Maintenance Resource Scheduling)

Can we activate Maintenance Resource Scheduling (43R) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602?

Also, is there any roadmap indication that RSH (Resource Scheduling for Maintenance Planners) will be discontinued or replaced in the future? Should customers instead consider SAP Field Service and Asset Management (SAP FSM) for maintenance scheduling requirements?

Based on the available documentation, 43R appears to be available in Public Cloud with an additional license. Could you please confirm the current SAP recommendation and long-term direction?

Regards,
Murari

entry.title: Re: DTW DOWNLOAD
entry.content: Great

entry.title: Creating Standard Profit Center Hierarchy in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (2602)
entry.content:
Introduction

Organizational hierarchies are the backbone of financial reporting in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. A well-structured Profit Center Hierarchy and Cost Center Hierarchy enables management reporting, cost allocation, profitability analysis, and internal controlling across your enterprise.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition release 2602, both hierarchies are maintained through SAP Fiori apps and Business Configuration (BC) activities — no more transaction codes like KCH5 or KSH1. There is no standard Migration Cockpit object available for migrating cost center or profit center hierarchies. This blog walks you through creating and maintaining both hierarchies from scratch, step by step.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure the following:

Requirement

Detail

SAP Role

Business Configuration Expert or Finance Configuration Specialist

Controlling Area

Must be created and assigned to Company Code

Fiscal Year Variant

Assigned to Controlling Area

 

Note: In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the Standard Hierarchy name is set during initial configuration and cannot be changed after profit centers or cost centers are created under it. Plan carefully before proceeding.

Part 1: Creating the Standard Profit Center Hierarchy

What Is a Profit Center Hierarchy?

A Profit Center Hierarchy (also called the Standard Hierarchy for Profit Centers) is a tree structure that organizes all your profit centers into logical groups — by region, business unit, product line, or any dimension meaningful to your business. SAP requires every profit center to belong to the standard hierarchy.

Step 1: Open Business Configuration

  1. Log in to your SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Search for the app "Business Configuration" or navigate via: Fiori Launchpad > Finance > Business Configuration
  3. In the search box, type: "Profit Center"
  4. Select the activity: "Define Standard Hierarchy for Profit Centers"

Step 2: Set the Standard Hierarchy Name

  1. Click "Edit" on the configuration activity.
  2. In the field Standard Hierarchy, enter a meaningful name. Example: PC_STD_HIER — Use uppercase, no spaces, max 12 characters.
  3. Click "Save".

Best Practice: Use a naming convention that reflects your company. For example, <CompanyCode>_PC_H -> 1000_PC_H

Step 3: Access the Manage Profit Center Groups App

  1. From the Fiori Launchpad, search for "Manage Profit Center Groups".
  2. Alternatively navigate to: Finance > Controlling > Profit Center Accounting > Manage Profit Center Groups
  3. The app opens showing the root node of your standard hierarchy.

Step 4: Create Hierarchy Root Node

  1. The Root Node is automatically created using the Standard Hierarchy name you defined (e.g., PC_STD_HIER).
  2. Click on the root node to expand it.
  3. You will see it is empty — ready for child nodes.

Step 5: Create Hierarchy Nodes (Groups)

Hierarchy nodes act as folders that group related profit centers.

Example Hierarchy Structure:

PC_STD_HIER  (Root)
|-- PC_AMERICAS
|   |-- PC_USA
|   +-- PC_CANADA
|-- PC_EUROPE
|   |-- PC_GERMANY
|   +-- PC_UK
+-- PC_APAC
    |-- PC_INDIA
    +-- PC_AUSTRALIA

To create a node:

  1. Click the root node to select it.
  2. Click "Create Child Node" (or the "+" icon).
  3. Enter the required fields (see table below).
  4. Click "Save".
  5. Repeat for each top-level node (e.g., PC_EUROPE, PC_APAC).

Field

Example Value

Description

Profit Center Group

PC_AMERICAS

Unique node ID

Description

Americas Region

Business-friendly label

Controlling Area

A000

Your controlling area

 

To create sub-nodes: click on the parent node, then "Create Child Node", fill in details, and save.

Step 6: Create Profit Centers and Assign to Hierarchy

  1. Search for "Manage Profit Centers" on the Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Click "Create".
  3. Fill in the required fields (see table below).
  4. Click "Save".

Field

Example Value

Note

Profit Center

PC_USA_EAST

Unique ID, up to 10 chars

Description

USA East Coast

Meaningful name

Controlling Area

A000

Same as hierarchy

Hierarchy Node

PC_USA

Select from hierarchy

Person Responsible

[User ID]

Optional but recommended

Profit Center Type

1

e.g., 1 = Revenue

Valid From

01.01.2024

Start date

Valid To

31.12.9999

Open-ended

Company Code

1000

Assign company code

Step 7: Verify the Profit Center Hierarchy

  1. Open "Manage Profit Center Groups".
  2. Navigate through the tree to confirm all nodes and profit centers are correctly placed.
  3. Use the "Search" function to locate individual profit centers.
  4. Cross-check: every profit center must appear as a leaf node under the hierarchy.

entry.title: Tax depreciation is not calculating correctly for assets acquired in the previous years
entry.content:
Hi Team,

We have configured Tax Depreciation Area 15 for Chart of Depreciation ZBG for Company Code 5108 (Bulgaria).

The tax depreciation key has been configured based on the depreciation rate (%) and useful life. In addition, an Evaluation Group (Tax Group) has been created to support tax depreciation calculation.

The tax depreciation is calculating correctly for assets acquired for current year 

However, for assets acquired in previous years, the tax depreciation is not being calculated correctly

we are looking for support or advise how to calculate the correct tax depreciation for assets acquired in previous years

Regards,

Prabhakar

entry.title: Re: Teaching AI Agents Best Practices: A Skills Workspace for CAP Development
entry.content:
Hi @StefanGraf 

Good question. Skills and ADRs operate at different layers but complement each other well:
- ADRs / architecture context constrain the solution space — "we chose X because Y." I keep these as project files (e.g. a simple CONTEXT.md) the agent reads at session start.
- Skills provide the execution playbook within those constraints — "given X, here's the one way to do it well."
Skills work regardless of the workflow — greenfield, brownfield, spec-driven or not. They're equally useful when building a new app from a spec as when adding a feature to an existing codebase.
Regarding a spec-driven approach in the SAP context SAP Joule Studio could be interesting (https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/joule-studio.html), which is heading in that direction — turning requirements into implementations with AI assistance. 
Best,
Daniel

entry.title: Re: Centralize Authorization Management of SAP Cloud ALM Using the Identity Provisioning Service
entry.content:
Hello Experts,      

I have a question about IPS for SAP Cloud ALM.

In my landscape:

  • Users are provisioned from Entra to IAS (Entra->IAS)
  • some users are created directly in IAS

Now I want to set up IPS to provision users from IAS to SAP Cloud ALM (BTP role collections).

I am not sure which source system I should use in IPS (IAS->CALM):

  • Identity Authentication (IAS)
    or
  • Local Identity Directory

In the documentation both options are mentioned, but it is not clear to me which one is recommended for SAP Cloud ALM.

Thank you very much.

Best Regard

entry.title: Re: Teaching AI Agents Best Practices: A Skills Workspace for CAP Development
entry.content:
Hi @MR3 ,

we are looking to build skills for both node and java and are testing a combined development skill (which checks if you are on node or java by looking at the project or asking the user in a new project) or by providing separate skills for both runtimes.

As you pointed out there is more node content out there - sample apps, tutorials etc. leading to better general coverage by the models. It means skills could be even more useful there for filling in the gaps.

I'm working towards releasing the skills library, hopefully I can give an update on that soon 😉

Thanks,

Daniel

entry.title: SAP Stammtisch | SAP Community Meetup (Dusseldorf #8)
entry.content:
Hallo Zusammen,

schon wieder ein Monat vorbei? Diese Woche Freitag gibt es den SAP CodeJam in Dortmund und direkt am Samstag den SAP Inside Track NRW, ebenfalls in Dortmund. Und dann? Am 19.06. laden wir euch dann nach Düsseldorf zum SAP Stammtisch #8 ein. Treffen ist wieder in der Brauerei Schumacher (Oststraße 123, 40210 Düsseldorf). Um 18 Uhr trifft sich die SAP Community zum Einstand ins Wochenende. Ob neu oder alt laden wir alle Interessierten ein, wir freuen uns auf euch.

Grüße

Orga-Team

###

Hi everyone,

has another month already flown by? This Friday, the SAP CodeJam is taking place in Dortmund, followed immediately on Saturday by the SAP Inside Track NRW, also in Dortmund. And what’s next? On June 19th, we invite you to Düsseldorf for SAP Community Meetup #8. We’ll be meeting once again at the Schumacher Brewery (Oststraße 123, 40210 Düsseldorf). The SAP community will gather at 6:00 PM to kick off the weekend. Whether you’re new to the scene or a long-time member, everyone interested is welcome. We look forward to seeing you there.

Best regards,

The Organizing Team

entry.title: Selection Profile - FU Filtering Condition from PO, SO, IDO, ODO
entry.content:
I am using SAP TM Basic License, and doing delivery based planning. When the inbound or outbound delivery gets created from PO or SO, the FU is getting generated.

In the selection profile, I would like to filter down the FU's using any one of the common field which is available in Sales Order, Purchase Order, Inbound Delivery and Outbound Delivery.

What is the field I can use it and how can I get FU filtered in selection profile.

entry.title: Re: IS-U to S/4HANA utilities - Architecture in LeanIX
entry.content:
Hi Ivo, Sure — happy to clarify.

When I say scope items, I’m referring to the standard SAP Best Practices / Process Navigator scope items that SAP publishes for S/4HANA Utilities. These are the fit‑to‑standard process packages (like Billing, Move‑In/Out, Meter Reading, FI‑CA processes, etc.) that SAP uses to define the end‑to‑end flows.

To give you a simple, sanitized example of how I usually structure it in LeanIX:

  • L1: S/4HANA Utilities

    • L2: Billing & Invoicing

      • L3: Billing Preparation

      • L3: Invoicing

      • L3: Bill Output

    • L2: Device Management

      • L3: Install/Remove Device

      • L3: Meter Reading

      • L3: Device Replacement

    • L2: FI‑CA

      • L3: Dunning

      • L3: Collections

      • L3: Payments

Nothing proprietary — just the structure.

So in short: Modules = L2 Scope Items / Best Practice processes = L3

This keeps the model clean and lines up well with SAP’s LeanIX guidelines.

If you want, I can also share a generic hierarchy template you can drop into draw.io or LeanIX.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
Correct initial balances
entry.content:
Hello.

In 12.2025 we loaded the opening balances of materials with movement 561. Now, in the middle of 2026, it turned out that only the quantities of some materials need to be adjusted by reduction while maintaining their value. The materials have an average valuation value. How can this be done most correctly in SAP?

Regards

Plamen K

entry.title: Re: Retail, fashion and vertical business in SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition 2508
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are currently running SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition and successfully using it for our Product and Asset Management processes. 

We are looking to expand our footprint to support Retail and Fashion operations. Since we are relatively new to the public cloud environment, we have a few questions regarding how to enable these industry-specific capabilities:

  1. Licensing & Subscription: Are the Retail and Fashion industry features inherently included in the standard SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition license, or do they require a separate subscription/add-on license? 
  2. Availability & Activation: Are these features built directly into the public cloud codebase? If yes, can they be activated via Scope Items in Central Business Configuration (CBC), or do they require a specific digital core extension?

Any guidance, documentation links, or Best Practices Explorer references would be highly appreciated!

Thanks
Khaza

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026 - SAP Autonomous Suite (What It Means for Consultants, Clients, and Executives)
entry.content:
Why SAP Autonomous Suite is different from consumer-grade generative AI

Most business users now understand generative AI through consumer-grade AI assistants. A user asks a question, gets a response, refines the prompt, and uses the output to write an email, summarize a document, build code, or analyze an idea. That experience is powerful, but it is mainly individual productivity AI.

SAP Autonomous Suite is different because it is designed for enterprise process execution. A person using a general-purpose AI tool may ask, “Can you summarize why this invoice is blocked?” The response may be helpful, but an enterprise finance team needs much more than a good explanation. It needs the system to know the vendor, purchase order, goods receipt, tax rule, tolerance limit, approval workflow, segregation-of-duties policy, and posting controls. It also needs traceability, authorization, auditability, and controlled execution. [3], [4]

That is where SAP’s business context becomes meaningful. Joule Assistants and Joule Agents are designed to operate with process context, not just language context. The difference is not simply a better answer. The difference is whether the system can help move a business process forward safely. [3], [7]

What SAP Autonomous Suite means for consultants

For consultants, SAP Autonomous Suite changes the nature of advisory work. The consultant’s role will not be limited to configuring transactions, preparing functional specifications, or building integrations. Consultants will increasingly need to design autonomous process patterns.

A traditional consultant may ask, “What transaction does the user execute, and what fields are required?” An autonomous-suite consultant must ask, “What business outcome is the user trying to achieve, what decisions are repetitive, what context is required, what can be automated, what must be approved, and what should remain inside the clean core?”

This creates new consulting responsibilities. Consultants must distinguish automation from autonomy, decompose end-to-end processes into decision points, and design trust controls such as role-based access, confidence thresholds, audit logs, exception routing, human-in-the-loop approvals, and clean-core write-back. [7]

What SAP Autonomous Suite means for clients

For clients, SAP Autonomous Suite should not be viewed as another feature upgrade. It should be viewed as a new operating model for business processes. Many enterprises still run processes that depend heavily on manual coordination across procurement, supply chain, finance, warehouse operations, and customer service. Each team may use SAP, but people still connect the dots manually.

The best starting points are business processes with high volume, repeatable decisions, measurable delays, and clear governance rules. Strong candidates include invoice processing, financial close, reconciliation, purchase requisitions, supplier selection, fulfillment exceptions, inventory imbalance, employee service requests, and customer service case handling.

The success factor for clients is readiness. Autonomous processes require clean data, stable master data, harmonized rules, well-defined APIs, cloud readiness, process ownership, and change management. AI will not fix a broken process; it will expose it faster.

What SAP Autonomous Suite means for executives

For executives, SAP Autonomous Suite should be understood as a business transformation lever, not a technical add-on. The executive question is not “How many AI agents can we deploy?” The better question is “Which business outcomes can we improve by redesigning work around intelligent, governed execution?” [1], [7]

For a CFO, this may mean reducing the financial close cycle, improving working capital, reducing invoice exceptions, and increasing audit confidence. For a COO or Chief Supply Chain Officer, it may mean reducing fulfillment delays, improving service levels, predicting supply disruptions earlier, and improving logistics productivity. For a CIO, it means building the platform foundation: clean core, BTP, Integration Suite, data governance, security, identity, observability, and responsible AI controls. [1], [7]

Real-world example 1: JK Cement and AI-driven purchase requisitions

A useful real-world example is JK Cement Ltd., one of India’s major cement producers. The company had a high-volume procurement environment with thousands of daily transactions, including more than a thousand purchase requisitions per day and thousands of invoices. Purchase requisition creation involved manual effort, complex screens, and multiple fields. [5]

Using SAP BTP and SAP Business AI, JK Cement created a conversational experience where employees could type purchase requests in plain English. The solution interprets the request, applies business logic, and creates the purchase requisition in SAP S/4HANA through governed integration. SAP reported 73% efficiency achieved in creating purchase requisitions and 50% greater time savings in purchase-related processes. [5]

This example shows the direction of SAP Autonomous Suite clearly. The user is not navigating a transaction manually. The user is expressing intent. The AI-enabled system interprets that intent, connects with enterprise data, applies business rules, and completes a structured business process. [5]

Consultant lesson: The work is not only chatbot design. It is process redesign, authorization design, integration design, validation logic, fallback handling, and change management.

Client lesson: Start with a high-volume process where users spend too much time entering structured information.

Executive lesson: The value is not only time savings. It is a scalable model for simplifying work across departments.

Real-world example 2: Western Sugar and autonomous invoice processing

Another strong example is Western Sugar Cooperative, which used SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to improve supplier invoice processing. Western Sugar handles approximately 40,000 supplier invoices annually. Many invoices arrived in paper or PDF format, creating manual processing effort, delays, and payment risk for growers and suppliers. [6]

SAP reported that Western Sugar processes 40,000 supplier invoices without human intervention and reduced invoice processing time by 25%. The AI capability embedded in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management helps process invoices faster, enabling the accounts payable team to focus more time on higher-value finance work. [6]

This is a practical illustration of autonomy in finance. The process is not simply digitizing an invoice. The system captures invoice data, validates it, routes it, supports approval, and posts it after approval. Human involvement is preserved where it matters, especially for approval and exception handling, but routine effort is reduced. [6]

Consultant lesson: Finance autonomy depends on process controls, tolerance rules, exception logic, and integration with the general ledger.

Client lesson: Invoice processing is a strong starting point because it has volume, measurable cycle time, and clear business impact.

Executive lesson: Autonomous finance is not just about reducing manual work. It improves control, visibility, supplier trust, and working capital discipline.

A practical architecture view

A practical SAP Autonomous Suite architecture can be understood through five layers: business execution, process and integration, data and context, intelligence, and governance. The clean-core principle remains essential. The ERP core should continue to handle transactional integrity: postings, ATP, pricing, delivery, invoicing, approvals, and financial records. The intelligence layer should reason, recommend, classify, summarize, and orchestrate. Write-back should happen only through approved APIs, workflows, and governed extensions. [1], [3], [7]

 

Final thoughts

SAP Autonomous Suite is not just another AI announcement. It is a signal that enterprise software is entering a new phase.

For consultants, this means learning how to design AI-enabled process patterns, not just configure applications. For clients, it means selecting business processes where autonomy can deliver measurable value without compromising control. For executives, it means treating AI as an operating model transformation, not a productivity experiment.

The most successful enterprises will not be the ones that deploy the most agents. They will be the ones that know where autonomy belongs, where human judgment must remain, and how to govern both within a clean, secure, and scalable SAP architecture.

SAP Autonomous Suite therefore represents a practical evolution of enterprise transformation: from transactions to outcomes, from automation to autonomy, and from isolated AI experiments to governed enterprise intelligence. [1], [7]

References

[1] SAP News Center, “SAP Unveils the Autonomous Enterprise,” 2026. URL: https://news.sap.com/2026/05/sap-sapphire-sap-unveils-autonomous-enterprise/

[2] SAP, “SAP Sapphire 2026 Innovation News Guide,” 2026. URL: https://www.sap.com/topics/events/sapphire/innovation-news-guide-2026

[3] SAP, “Joule Agents and Joule Assistants,” 2026. URL: https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-agents.html

[4] SAP, “Joule Business AI Solutions,” 2026. URL: https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence.html

[5] SAP, “JK Cement: Laying a scalable foundation for fast, intuitive processes driven by intelligent automation with SAP Business AI,” 2025. URL: https://www.sap.com/asset/dynamic/2025/09/72b8b6e8-1f7f-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

[6] SAP, “Western Sugar: Automating invoice processing with AI to keep growers and suppliers super sweet,” 2025. URL: https://www.sap.com/asset/dynamic/2025/02/4282b6dc-f67e-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

[7] SAP News Center, “SAP Unveils Business AI Platform to Power the Autonomous Enterprise,” 2026. URL: https://news.sap.com/2026/05/sap-sapphire-keynote-business-ai-platform-power-autonomous-enterprise/

entry.title: SaaS Is Not Dead, It’s Just Evolving: 5 Surprising Shifts Redefining the Future of Work
entry.content:
1. The End of the "Fragmented" Enterprise

For years, digital transformation has been hindered by a frustrating reality: the modern enterprise operates as a collection of fragmented systems, disconnected processes, and siloed reports. In typical operations, planning passes to procurement, which passes to manufacturing, which eventually reaches logistics. Each manual handoff introduces delay, forcing leaders to reconstruct reality from outdated data long after a decision was required.

We are now entering the era of the Autonomous Enterprise. This is not merely a buzzword; it is an end-state where AI manages and executes end-to-end processes proactively. While a current industry narrative suggests that "SaaS is dead"—arguing that AI agents will render systems of record obsolete—the reality is a sophisticated evolution. Software is not disappearing; it is becoming the "governed context" for AI.

Think of the modern SaaS stack as the operating system for AI. Without the deep knowledge of how finance connects to procurement or the specific compliance rules governing a transaction, AI cannot reliably run a business. Software provides the "frame of validation rules" and enforceable governance that ensures an AI doesn't just act, but acts within the boundaries of enterprise accountability.

2. From "Click-and-Search" to "Intent-Driven" Engagement

The way we interact with enterprise software is undergoing a fundamental shift toward an "app-less" experience. Traditional navigation, characterized by clicking through dozens of static screens to find a tool, is being replaced by Joule Work. This new engagement layer allows users to move away from searching for functions and toward expressing "intent."

In this fluid information architecture, the system translates a user’s objective into a governed execution path. Crucially, Joule Work is designed as a "headless" capability. While it provides a first-class SAP experience, the same intelligence and orchestration are surfaced in third-party enterprise tools, AI workstations, and custom-built solutions where users already spend their time.

"Joule Work is the persistent, app-free workspace... where users express goals and the system translates intent into coordinated, end-to-end execution. Together, they replace fragmented navigation with a single, governed environment for getting enterprise work done."

 

3. The Rise of the "Teammate" AI: Assistants vs. Agents

In the Autonomous Enterprise, work is handled through a sophisticated collaboration between two distinct AI roles:

  • AI Assistants (The Teammates): These are role-based coordinators. They understand the context of a specific job—like a recruiter or a controller—and coordinate the necessary tools and people to get work done.
  • AI Agents (The Doers): These are autonomous executors designed to perform multi-step tasks across systems. The true breakthrough lies in multi-agent orchestration, where agents delegate to other agents, creating a coordinated network that handles complex workflows without step-by-step human instructions.

A core principle of this evolution is "Human-in-the-loop by default." The goal is not "lights-out" automation, but "Supervised Autonomy." Humans remain in the lead for critical decisions, while AI handles the "busy work." For example, in Autonomous Adaptive Production, a network of agents coordinates planning, engineering, procurement, and logistics simultaneously. A signal anywhere in the chain—like a demand spike—triggers a coordinated response:

  • Autonomous Finance: Real-time reconciliation and cross-functional risk signaling.
  • Autonomous SCM: Automatic production adjustments and inventory reallocation.
  • Autonomous Spend: Proactive risk identification and untapped savings discovery.

4. Why "Generic AI" Fails Where "Industry AI" Succeeds

Generic Large Language Models (LLMs) often lack the business logic required for enterprise reliability. They generate fluent text but fail to respect inventory constraints or regulatory policies. Industry AI solves this by embedding sector-specific process logic and regulatory requirements directly into agentic solutions.

This works because of the SAP Knowledge Graph, which transforms disconnected data into a semantic network of meaning, and SAP RPT-1, a model designed specifically for structured tabular business data that requires no traditional model training.

Generic AI

SAP Industry AI

Isolated at the "edge" without business context.

Grounded in 50 years of encoded process knowledge.

Limited understanding of relational business data.

Uses SAP Knowledge Graph to link customers, contracts, and logic.

Lacks built-in governance and validation rules.

Integrated governance with audit trails and compliance checks.

Requires manual "bolting on" to systems.

Uses SAP RPT-1 for instant predictive modeling of tabular data.

5. The "T-Shirt Sizing" of Enterprise Transformation

To make the vision of an autonomous enterprise practical and deployable, SAP utilizes Autonomous Domain Blueprints. These are modular frameworks that package data, AI features, and services into a consumable offering for specific domains.

To simplify deployment, these blueprints use a "T-shirt sizing" (S, M, L) approach based on the number of users and the volume of agent activity. This turns a complex technical roadmap into a predictable operational reality. This approach is managed via the Autonomous Domain Configurator, the operational bridge planned for Q3 2026 that allows leaders to configure their path to autonomy. These blueprints target measurable outcomes; for instance, Autonomous Asset Management is designed to deliver:

  • 10–30% reduction in unplanned downtime.
  • 5–30% reduction in total maintenance costs.
  • 10–30% reduction in working capital.

6. Agent-Led Transformation: Making the Move 35% Faster

The journey to the cloud is no longer a manual, resource-intensive hurdle. Within RISE with SAP, an Agent-led toolchain is redefining the migration process. By automating time-intensive activities, organizations can achieve a Clean Core—a standardized ERP environment that is easy to update and ready for continuous innovation.

Seven new migration assistants (scheduled for Q3-Q4 2026) turn the transformation into a guided, data-driven journey: System Analysis, Data Management, Custom Code, Configuration, Test Management, Rollout, and Project Management.

"Powered by Agent-led toolchain and backed by the RISE with SAP Methodology, this approach reduces effort and time, making the path to the cloud at least 35% more efficient."

7. Conclusion: A New Era of Supervised Collaboration

The software stack is being reshaped, not destroyed. In this new era, applications and data provide the governed context within which AI can act reliably. We are moving toward an "event-driven collaboration" where AI handles the massive scale of routine execution and humans provide the strategic judgment and accountability.

As the Autonomous Enterprise takes over the routine "busy work" of the organization, a fundamental question remains for every business leader: As AI takes over the busy work, where will your team’s strategic judgment drive the most value?

My other blog posts: Think You Know Enterprise Architecture? These 5 Insights Will Change Your Mind

entry.title: Re: SWAPS ST02
entry.content: Catalog Buffer rsdb/cua_buffer_size = 3000 rsdb/obj/buffersize = 160317 Program Buffer abap/buffersize = Screen Buffer rsdb/ntab/entrycount = 2568192 Memory em/initial_size_MB = 12525 Roll & Heap control ztta/roll_extension = 4000000000 abap/heap_area_dia = 2000000000 abap/heap_area_nondia = 2000000000

entry.title: Re: SaaS Is Not Dead, It’s Just Evolving: 5 Surprising Shifts Redefining the Future of Work
entry.content: Well said. The future isn't about replacing enterprise systems with AI—it's about AI operating within trusted business context. The combination of governed data, business processes, and autonomous agents is what will make the Autonomous Enterprise a reality. Joule Work represents a major shift from application-centric workflows to intent-driven execution.

entry.title: Re: SAP Community Voice: New SAP Champions, Vote Badges Colors and SAP Integration Suite highlights
entry.content: Welcome

entry.title: Re: Create an own (Z*) Function-Module within an SAP-Owne...
entry.content:
If SAP were to introduce a new function module to this function group in a future release, and it were to use the include LM61QU20, this could create compatibility issues during subsequent system updates. Such modifications to standard SAP components are generally discouraged as they may:

  1. Be overwritten during standard SAP upgrades
  2. Cause technical conflicts with future SAP enhancements
  3. Require extensive regression testing after each SAP update

As a best practice, I recommend:

  1. Creating a Z-namespace function group for custom developments
  2. Using enhancement points or BAdIs where possible
  3. Documenting any necessary modifications to standard components

entry.title: Re: Centralize Authorization Management of SAP Cloud ALM Using the Identity Provisioning Service
entry.content:
I would use Local Identity Directory as the Source System type - IAS uses the SCIM API of the CIS Tenant to read from the Identity Directory. Local Identity Directory reads directly from the Identity Directory of the CIS tenant. One less set of credentials to manage.

Cheers,
Sebastian

entry.title: Re: Tax code is not reflecting in work order
entry.content: Could you describe your S/4HANA MM process: flow with tax code, purchase order, work order ?

entry.title: In BNK_MONI, we are seeing two payment files generated
entry.content: In BNK_MONI, we are seeing two payment files generated in the Data Medium Overview for a single batch. Could you please explain the reason for this duplication and advise where the configuration should be adjusted to ensure only one payment file is created

entry.title: Re: NEW COMPANY CODE CREATION WITH DIFFERANT CURRENCY IN SAME INSTANCE
entry.content: Please explain what is the challenge you are facing in configuring this

entry.title: Artificial Intelligence & SAP EPPM
entry.content:
AI is fundamentally reshaping SAP Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management (EPPM) by enabling intelligent automation, predictive analytics, and data-driven decision-making across the full project lifecycle. By integrating capabilities such as machine learning, natural language processing, and generative AI, SAP EPPM is evolving from a traditional project tracking platform into a proactive, intelligent management system — helping organizations align project investments with strategic objectives, optimize resources, and mitigate risks before they escalate.

Key AI Capabilities in SAP EPPM

 

  • Intelligent Project Navigation (NLP): The Joule Copilot's Navigational Project Assistant uses natural language processing to allow users to find and open applications displaying project data, directly access projects, Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) elements, or networks in the Project Builder — reducing navigation effort and improving user satisfaction. 
  • AI-Driven Resource Skill Matching: The Advanced Skill Match feature in SAP Project and Resource Management (SPRM), powered by SAP Business AI, automates skill matching and similarity analysis — identifying candidates with exact or similar competencies and ranking them by relevance and availability. 
  • Predictive Risk & Early Warning Detection: Agentic AI can detect early warning signals across project variables, helping teams mitigate risk before issues escalate. 
  • Automated Portfolio Scoring & Prioritization: AI-powered solutions (e.g., built on SAP BTP integrated with large language models) automate the scoring, simulation, and prioritization of project portfolios, replacing subjective manual scoring with data-driven evaluation. 

How AI Enhances Core EPPM Processes

Portfolio Management

AI enables dynamic portfolio prioritization by aligning project plans with strategic objectives. Solutions built on SAP BTP can automate portfolio scoring and simulate project outcomes under different conditions, empowering finance teams and portfolio managers to make informed decisions about which initiatives to pursue, postpone, or cancel. This reduces governance bottlenecks and improves financial transparency.

Risk & Issue Management

A unified data foundation within SAP EPPM enables Agentic AI to dynamically prioritize portfolios and detect early warning signals across project variables — assisting teams to mitigate risk proactively before issues escalate. Additionally, machine learning-based scenarios (such as Risk of Late Payment) predict which customers are most likely to miss payment deadlines, with AI explainability features available to analyze scenario trends. 

Joule Copilot

Intelligent AI assistant embedded in SAP Fiori launchpad for project navigation, planning, and sales order monitoring

SAP Project and Resource Management (SPRM)

Cloud-based resource management tool with AI-driven skill matching for ETO and PSA projects

Business Benefits

  1. Enhanced Efficiency: Faster staffing processes through AI-driven skill matching reduce project delays and administrative overhead. 
  2. Improved Decision-Making: Automated portfolio scoring and live simulations enable more informed, data-driven investment decisions — reducing governance bottlenecks. 
  3. Proactive Risk Mitigation: Early warning signal detection across project variables allows teams to address risks before they escalate into issues. 
  4. Improved Resource Utilization: AI aligns workforce capabilities with project needs, ensuring the right people are assigned to the right roles. 
  5. Operational Cost Reduction: Organizations implementing AI-enhanced SAP solutions have experienced considerable reductions in operational costs within the first year of deployment, stemming from automation of labor-intensive processes and reduction of manual errors. 
  6. Better User Experience: Natural language navigation via Joule reduces effort for accessing project data and improves overall user satisfaction.

Future Outlook

  • Agentic AI is emerging as a key direction for SAP EPPM, with intelligent agents capable of dynamically prioritizing project portfolios, aligning plans with strategic objectives, and detecting early warning signals autonomously
  • SAP AI Agents focused on sales, supply chain optimization, pricing strategies, and inventory management are upcoming additions to SAP's AI ecosystem.

"AI is transforming SAP EPPM from a passive project tracking tool into an intelligent, proactive management platform that supports the full project and portfolio lifecycle"

entry.title: Re: Content Transport Using SAP Cloud Transport Management Service for SAP CI CF Environment
entry.content:
Hi @Arpan_2401 ,

Error messages clearly says something wrong in destination. Kindly verify step 4 to 7 and recheck.

FYI - The destination name is case sensitive.

Regards,

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Correct initial balances
entry.content:
For the adjustment of material quantities where only the stock quantities need to be reduced while maintaining the existing valuation, I recommend the following SAP-compliant procedure:

  • Quantity Adjustment via Physical Inventory Movement:

Execute a physical inventory adjustment using movement type 562 

  • Valuation Compensation via Price Change:

Utilize transaction MR21 (Mass Change of Stock) with the "Price Change" function
This will allow for the necessary valuation adjustments to compensate for the quantity changes.

  • Both transactions should be executed with the same valuation date

entry.title: Re: Error in PUP price for Material:PPRSSFB-5KG-RS400
entry.content: Did you verify via MM03 > Environment > Display Changes or MM04 ?

entry.title: Re: Content Transport Using SAP Cloud Transport Management Service for SAP CI CF Environment
entry.content:
Hi @bharathrepati ,

Error messages clearly says something wrong in destination. Kindly verify step 4 to 7 and recheck.

FYI - The destination name is case sensitive.

Regards,

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: SAP Secure Login Client on macOS – plans for Apple Si...
entry.content: See this SAP Note from 2022: 3151785 - SAP SSO Fixes for Secure Login Client 3.0 SP 02 Patch 13

entry.content:
Hi sir all destination configurations are correct content assembly service, transport management service these are automatically filed by selecting instance name i cross verified all destinations manually and with the help of AI  agents, and right now i am using btp trial account so AI agents are saying that trial accounts are restricted mtar file generation.

Sir can you come with solution.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 04 - Namespaces, contexts and name prefixes
entry.content: 99f5602345d2d1c3ab370bd8bbbc7e8d9a003b67d9ddabcdc4f8f46ab8ed52c0

entry.title: Re: SWAPS ST02
entry.content:
Based on the current configuration and observed swap statistics, I would recommend the following optimization steps:

Increase Extended Memory
Parameter:

em/initial_size_MB

Current value:

12525

Recommended value:

18000 – 24000

Please ensure sufficient physical RAM is available on the operating system before increasing this parameter.

This adjustment is considered the most important optimization step, as insufficient Extended Memory can force work processes into Heap memory usage, potentially leading to OS swap pressure and ST02 buffer swaps.

Reduce excessive Heap allocation
Parameter:

abap/heap_area_dia

Current value:

2000000000

Recommended value:

1000000000

Reason:

Prevent excessive DIA work process memory consumption
Prioritize Extended Memory usage
Reduce operating system memory pressure
Improve overall memory stability


Increase Program Buffer
Parameter:

abap/buffersize

Current value:

2568192

Recommended value:

3500000 – 4500000

This can help reduce program reloads and buffer swaps, especially in systems with extensive custom developments or high workload activity.

Increase Catalog Buffer
Parameter:

rsdb/obj/buffersize

Current value:

160317

Recommended value:

250000 – 350000

This adjustment is particularly recommended due to the high Catalog Buffer swap statistics observed in ST02.

After implementing the changes, the SAP instance should be restarted and monitored for at least 24–48 hours.

Very Important Next Check

You MUST check OS memory.

In ST06 or Linux:

free -g
vmstat 1
top

Especially check:

  • swap used
  • free RAM
  • paging activity

Because:

If OS swap is already active,
increasing buffers may worsen the situation.

entry.content: probably your s4 still have separated gateway

entry.title: Re: Posting not possible (Transaction type not allowed in...
entry.content: This is not a Program error but process mistake it seems and this has been confirmed in SAP note 3130895. If the symptoms listed there is not matching with your issue, then raise a ticket to SAP referring this SAP note

entry.title: SuccessFactors: Provide Admins and HR access to Growth Portfolio Team View
entry.content:
Hello,

how can I provide access to the Team View within Growth Portfolio to Admins and HR to view the data of employees? I have tried everything within the role assignment but within the people search no user appears when searching for one which is definetely part of the target group. Yes, I have included the permission to view on my self.

Thanks, Silke

entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud Dimension Tables Move to the File Repository
entry.content:
Hi @MScherer ,

the file repository folder "Public > Dimension Tables" is needed for a common place where to put all the public dimensions during migration. After the migration you are free to move the dimensions to other folders as you like, but keep in mind that this might result in different privileges, depending on how the new target folder is configured. So in the end, the "Dimension Tables" folder might be empty, however it should not be deleted since it is also used when importing legacy packages containing public dimensions.

Regards, Stefan

entry.title: Re: Issues with Build Process Automation project becoming unresponsive
entry.content: In my case it's different projects and only one user.

entry.title: Re: VBOF showing error Log for Agreement 61634
entry.content:
The error “System status CLSD is active” during VBOF means that the rebate agreement you are trying to update (61634) has already been closed in the system. Once an agreement is in status CLSD, SAP prevents further postings or updates to sales volume, which is why VBOF cannot proceed.

The error is standard SAP control. To run VBOF successfully, the rebate agreement must be in open status. If business policy allows, reopen via VBO2; otherwise, create a new agreement for future postings.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 05 - Portable functions
Re: Issues with Build Process Automation project becoming unresponsive
entry.content:
Restarting the agent doesn't help, no upgrade needed.
I can confirm that testing using different browsers hasn't caused any issues today, this could be a partial solution.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 08 - CXL case-when-then operator
Universidad Madero Wins First Place in the SAP Signavio LAC Contest 2026
entry.content:
Universidad Madero Wins First Place in the SAP Signavio LAC Contest 2026

The SAP Signavio LAC Contest 2026 brought together university students from across Latin America and the Caribbean to solve real-world Business Process Management (BPM) challenges using SAP Signavio. With 179 registrations and six finalist teams representing leading universities in the region, the competition showcased innovation, analytical thinking, and collaboration among the next generation of business and technology professionals.

After weeks of dedication, learning, and teamwork, the first-place award was presented to Universidad Madero (UMAD), Mexico. The second and third places were awarded to Universidad de la Ciudad de Buenos Aires (Argentina) and Universidad ICESI (Colombia), respectively.

 

Learning Through Real-World Challenges

The contest challenged students to apply BPM concepts and SAP Signavio capabilities to a real business case. Participants received specialized training sessions and worked collaboratively to analyze processes, identify improvement opportunities, and present innovative solutions.

For many students, the competition represented their first experience with SAP technologies. Carolina Reyes Pitones, a student from UMAD, shared:

“This was my first encounter with SAP tools, and it was an enriching experience. Although the contest coincided with the end of our semester, I learned a great deal and finished the experience motivated to continue exploring SAP solutions and participate in future challenges.”

Maria José Espinosa Minjares highlighted the support provided throughout the competition:

“Being part of the SAP Signavio LAC Contest 2026 was a unique and enriching experience. The three-day training course helped us understand the platform and gave us confidence to develop our proposal. The challenge taught me how to communicate better with my team, collaborate effectively, and leverage each member’s strengths.”

 

A Victory Built on Commitment and Teamwork

For Professor Martha Patricia Hernández Zamora, who mentored the winning team, the achievement reflects not only technical skills but also dedication and perseverance.

“We are extremely excited to have won first place. The contest took place during final exam season, requiring students to balance their academic responsibilities with the project. Watching their commitment, creativity, and knowledge throughout the process was incredibly rewarding.”

Professor Hernández also emphasized the educational value of the challenge and the impact of SAP Signavio on student learning:

“The platform encouraged students to develop their skills and reflect on the strategic importance of business processes. It was inspiring to see them compare their abilities with students from other universities across the region and demonstrate precision, creativity, and strong analytical thinking.”

She also recognized the long-standing collaboration between Universidad Madero and SAP University Alliances, which has supported students since 2012.

 

Celebrating Excellence Across Latin America

The success of the SAP Signavio LAC Contest 2026 was made possible through the combined efforts of students, professors, SAP experts, judges, and partners committed to developing future BPM professionals.

A special recognition goes to Professor Alfredo Armijos-De La Cruz, who designed the competition and delivered the hands-on SAP Signavio training sessions that prepared participants to compete at the highest level. His dedication and expertise helped transform academic concepts into practical BPM experience for students across the region.

We also extend our sincere thanks to our partner Edison and its team for their collaboration, industry expertise, and commitment to supporting the next generation of BPM professionals in Latin America and the Caribbean. Their contribution added valuable real-world perspectives and enriched the learning experience for all participants.

We congratulate all finalist teams and especially the winners from Universidad Madero for their outstanding achievement. Their success demonstrates how academic excellence, practical learning, and collaboration can create meaningful impact and prepare students for the challenges of the digital business world.

We look forward to welcoming even more talented students in future editions of the SAP Signavio LAC Contest.

 

Winners

🥇1st Place: Universidad Madero (Mexico)
🥈2nd Place: Universidad de la Ciudad de Buenos Aires (Argentina)
🥉3rd Place: ICESI (Colombia)

 

Related LinkedIn Posts

  • Martha Patricia Hernández Zamora: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/paty-hdez-82b30b31_estamos-profundamente-honrados-de-haber-alcanzado-activity-7467654710875979776-CFDx
  • María José Espinosa Minjares: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/activity-7466276393681612800-sHDP

entry.title: Re: NEW COMPANY CODE CREATION WITH DIFFERANT CURRENCY IN SAME INSTANCE
entry.content:
Are there any specific problems or errors you've encountered in the Maintain Company Codes application?

NB: Could you please avoid using all capital letters in your messages? It makes the text harder to read and can be perceived as aggressive.

entry.title: Re: Supply chain management
entry.content:
Since you did not mention how many years of experience you have, I would suggest:

  • If you’re early in your career (1–3 years), start with MM and SD basics.
  • If you have more experience (5+ years), aim directly for EWM and integration topics, which are in high demand.

entry.title: TSI University Wins First Place in the SAP Signavio EMEA Student Contest 2026
entry.content:
TSI University Wins First Place in the SAP Signavio EMEA Student Contest 2026

The SAP Signavio EMEA Student Contest 2026 brought together students from across Europe, the Middle East, and Africa to solve real-world Business Process Management (BPM) challenges using SAP Signavio. After weeks of learning, collaboration, and problem-solving, TSI University from Latvia emerged as the competition's first-place winner, demonstrating outstanding analytical thinking, innovation, and process transformation expertise.

With 161 registrations from 18 countries, the contest created a truly international environment where students applied BPM concepts to realistic business scenarios and gained hands-on experience with SAP Signavio.

 

A Winning Experience for TSI University

For the team from TSI University, the competition was much more than a challenge, it was an opportunity to develop practical consulting skills while collaborating in a global academic community.

Anna Manakova, a member of the winning team, shared:

“Participating in the SAP Signavio EMEA Contest 2026 was a highly valuable and inspiring experience for me and my team. During the competition, we had the opportunity to work on real-life tasks by modelling the current state of business processes, analysing company operations, and identifying more effective solutions using SAP Signavio tools.”

She also emphasized the international nature of the event:

“Students and teams from different countries around the world took part in the contest, creating a truly international environment for the exchange of ideas and professional growth. We are very grateful to SAP for the opportunity to take part in this challenge, gain practical experience, and test our skills in the role of business process consultants.”

The achievement reflects the team's dedication, problem-solving capabilities, and ability to apply BPM methodologies to real business challenges. Competing against talented students from across the EMEA region, TSI University showcased the skills and innovative mindset that future business process professionals need to succeed in today's digital economy.

 

Bringing Business Process Management to Life

Throughout the competition, students stepped into the role of business process consultants and were challenged to:

  • Model current-state (As-Is) business processes
  • Identify inefficiencies and improvement opportunities
  • Design future-state (To-Be) processes
  • Explore AI and automation opportunities using SAP Signavio

To support participants, SAP experts delivered eight hours of hands-on training, helping students build practical BPM skills and apply them to real-world scenarios.

 

Celebrating Excellence Across EMEA

The quality of submissions demonstrated the remarkable talent of students across the region. Congratulations to all finalists and winners:

🥇1st Place — TSI University (Latvia)
🥈2nd Place — Azerbaijan State Oil and Industry University (Azerbaijan)
🥉3rd Place — University of Cape Town (South Africa)

 

A Community Effort

The SAP Signavio EMEA Student Contest 2026 was made possible thanks to the dedication of students, professors, trainers, judges, and industry partners committed to developing the next generation of BPM professionals.

Special thanks to bpmd and Liam O'Neill for their partnership, as well as Weston Washington and Jason D. Harris for delivering the practical training sessions that prepared participants for the challenge.

Congratulations once again to TSI University and all participating teams for bringing theory to life and demonstrating the transformative power of Business Process Management.

 

Related LinkedIn Post

Anna Manakova (TSI University, Latvia):
https://www.linkedin.com/posts/anna-manakova-065767320_sapsignavio-academicpartnerships-studentschallenge-activity-7466068954101493760-y4cL

entry.title: Live Connection from SAC to Datasphere
entry.content:
Dear SAP, 

we are trying to create a live connection from our productive SAC Systems to our productive new Datasphere tenant. 

We are getting an error message regarding oAuth. See Screenshot attached. 

the system is maintained as a trusted system in the sac. 

we do not have a formation, here another ticket is open. 

we are trying a direct connection. 

best regards

Marc Schweizer

entry.title: Re: Automate Job Requisition Updates from Position Data Using Integration Center
entry.content:
Hi @robinkumar15 

Thank you for Posting this in detail.

In the filter section, for below step mentioned

"I want the requisition to be updated only if the linked position has been modified. To achieve this, I have set a filter to compare the 'Change Date' of the std_position_obj within the requisition against the last run date/time."

 

I cannot able to see Std_position_obj to select the change date.

 

Can you please help me on this.

entry.title: Factoring AI into Your Procurement Strategy
entry.content:
Driving Real Procurement Transformation: Beyond Software and AI 🌐

In a recent episode of SAP’s Future of Procurement podcast, Jay Brand, Director of Solution Advisory for Spend and Finance, shared candid insights into what truly drives successful procurement transformations. While technology—especially AI—dominates today’s headlines, the conversation underscored a critical truth: transformation is not delivered by software alone. It is achieved through clarity of purpose, strong leadership, disciplined execution, and ongoing commitment to improvement. 🚀

Software Isn’t Magic—Strategy Comes First 🧭

A central theme in the discussion was the misconception that software alone can deliver transformation. Organizations often invest heavily in technology expecting immediate results, only to be disappointed when outcomes fall short. As Brand highlighted, tools are only as effective as the strategy behind them.

True transformation starts with a clear understanding of business objectives, measurable KPIs, and the organizational will to change. Without this foundation, even the most advanced solutions become underutilized. Leaders must define the “why” before investing in the “how,” ensuring alignment across stakeholders and a shared vision for success. 📊

Avoiding the Feature Trap ⚙️

Another common pitfall is the tendency to get lost in feature-level comparisons during solution evaluation. Teams often focus on minor system differences—interface preferences or configuration details—rather than assessing how a platform supports broader business goals.

Brand emphasized that this “feature-first” mindset can derail transformation efforts. Instead, organizations should prioritize outcomes over perfection, avoiding analysis paralysis. Decision-making should be streamlined, guided by a small group of empowered stakeholders, and focused on achieving measurable value rather than replicating legacy processes. 🔍

Transformation Is a Continuous Journey 🌱

One of the most compelling analogies shared during the podcast likened transformation to caring for a plant. Organizations invest heavily during implementation—watering, nurturing, and monitoring progress. However, once systems go live, attention often fades, leading to stagnation or failure.

Successful companies recognize that transformation doesn’t end at go-live. Continuous improvement, regular optimization, and ongoing user engagement are critical. Cloud solutions, in particular, require iterative enhancements, frequent reviews, and adaptation to evolving business needs. Maintaining this momentum ensures long-term value realization. 🔄

AI: Powerful, but Not a Shortcut 🤖

The rise of artificial intelligence has introduced both opportunity and unrealistic expectations. While AI holds significant promise for procurement, Brand stressed that it is not a “magic bullet.” Organizations expecting AI to instantly solve complex challenges often overlook the foundational work required.

Effective AI adoption begins with clearly defined problems. Companies must articulate specific use cases rather than asking broad questions like “What can AI do for us?” Without this clarity, AI initiatives risk becoming unfocused and ineffective. 🎯

Data: The Foundation of AI Success 📈

A recurring insight was the critical role of data in unlocking AI’s potential. Clean, structured, and interconnected data is essential for meaningful insights and automation. Many organizations have accumulated large volumes of data but lack the strategy to manage or leverage it effectively.

Brand emphasized that data quality—not just quantity—determines AI success. Poor data leads to inaccurate outputs, which can have serious business consequences, especially in areas like forecasting or supply chain planning. Establishing a robust data strategy is therefore a prerequisite for any AI-driven transformation. 🧠

Integrated Ecosystems Over Fragmented Solutions 🔗

When considering procurement transformation, organizations face a choice between best-of-breed tools and integrated solution suites. The podcast highlighted a growing trend toward integrated ecosystems, particularly in the age of AI.

Unified platforms provide consistent data models and seamless process integration, enabling more advanced analytics and automation. This holistic approach enhances visibility, reduces complexity, and supports enterprise-wide optimization. While niche solutions may address specific needs, they often lack the broader context required for strategic decision-making. 🏗️

Balancing Innovation with Realism ⚖️

A key takeaway was the importance of setting realistic expectations. While innovation is essential, organizations must recognize that enterprise-grade solutions evolve at a measured pace. Providers are balancing cutting-edge capabilities with reliability, scalability, and business-critical accuracy.

Transparent communication between vendors and customers is vital. By setting clear expectations, demonstrating current capabilities, and outlining a roadmap for future innovation, organizations can build trust and ensure alignment throughout the transformation journey. 🤝

The Future of Procurement: Evolution, Not Revolution 🔮

Looking ahead, Brand offered a pragmatic perspective on the future of procurement. While technology will continue to evolve, the core principles of procurement—relationship management, strategic sourcing, and value creation—will remain constant.

AI and automation will augment, not replace, human expertise. Procurement professionals will continue to play a critical role in decision-making, supplier relationships, and strategic planning. The future lies in blending advanced technology with human insight to drive sustainable value. 👥

Final Thoughts 💡

Procurement transformation is not a one-time initiative but an ongoing journey requiring strategic clarity, disciplined execution, and continuous improvement. Technology and AI are powerful enablers, but their success depends on foundational elements—clear goals, strong data, and organizational commitment.

As organizations navigate this evolving landscape, the winners will be those who balance ambition with realism, leveraging innovation while staying grounded in core business principles. The question is not whether to transform—but how thoughtfully and effectively that transformation is pursued. ✅

Listen on APPLE!

Listen on Spotify!

entry.title: Re: SAP ERP Public Cloud Edition parallel line
entry.content:
Hi @Cornelia07 ,

Here are your Questions:

Can scope items still be activated after the parallel line has been created? And can new plants, for example, be added? Both, of course, in the main line and the parallel line.

The answer is yes. But I should point out that you need to take into the consideration of using PL strategically: keeping ML stable, and adding new things in PL, then merging them back to ML. With that in mind, I suggest you adding new scopes, new plants, etc. in PL first, and then merging them to ML. Don't add them simultaneously on both lines.

Best,

George
